name: Ad Hominem
subtitle: Attack on the Person
summary: Attacking the person rather than their arguments. One of the oldest logical fallacies.
definition: Ad hominem (Latin: 'against the man') is a logical fallacy in which one attacks the character, motives, or other personal attributes of someone making an argument, rather than addressing the substance of the argument itself.
historicalContext: Classified by Aristotle among the sophisms. Systematically employed by totalitarian regimes: the label 'enemy of the people' delegitimized any argument regardless of its merit.
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits the source heuristic: we tend to evaluate information based on who delivers it. When the source is delegitimized, the brain automatically discards even sound arguments.

variants.name: Abusive
variants.desc: Direct insults: 'You're an idiot!'

variants.name: Circumstantial
variants.desc: Attack on interests: 'You're paid to say that!'

variants.name: Tu Quoque
variants.desc: Accusation of hypocrisy: 'You do the same thing!'

variants.name: Guilt by Association
variants.desc: Guilt by association: 'They talk with X, therefore...'

redFlags: The argument focuses on the person making the claim, not on the claim itself
Repeated use of negative labels
No engagement with the specific content of the argument

caseStudies.title: Galileo
caseStudies.description: He was branded a 'heretic' rather than having his astronomical arguments refuted.
caseStudies.lesson: Personal flaws do not invalidate arguments.

defense.action: Identify when the discussion shifts from the facts to the person
Isolate the argument from any personal attributes
'What does this have to do with the specific proposal?'

scenario.title: Energy policy debate
scenario.situation: An engineer proposes nuclear power plants. An opponent says: 'We can't listen to him! He works for an industry company!'
scenario.question: Which variant is being used?

options.explanation: It attacks the person's presumed interests to avoid discussing the technical merits.

options.text: Legitimate concern about conflicts of interest
options.explanation: Conflicts of interest should be disclosed, but they do not automatically invalidate an argument.
There are no direct insults.

name: Ridicule
subtitle: The Weapon of Mockery
summary: Turning the opponent into an object of derision to delegitimize them without argument.
definition: Systematic ridicule uses humor to make it socially costly to take the opponent seriously.
historicalContext: Used by regimes through caricatures of ethnic groups and opponents. In the digital age, memes have become powerful weapons.
psychologicalMechanism: Humor creates an emotional association that bypasses reasoning. Someone who laughs at another person categorizes them as 'not worthy of serious consideration.'

variants.name: Serial memes
variants.desc: Coordinated campaigns targeting the same figure

variants.name: Selective montages
variants.desc: Compilations of out-of-context blunders

variants.name: Derogatory nicknames
variants.desc: Humorous labels used in place of the person's name

redFlags: Memes targeting physical appearance rather than ideas
Systematic one-sided satire
Mockery without any accompanying argument

caseStudies.title: The power of memes
caseStudies.description: Studies show that candidates systematically ridiculed in memes are perceived as less competent.
caseStudies.lesson: Memes are political communication with measurable effects.

defense.action: 'It makes me laugh' does not mean 'they are wrong'
Who produces the content that makes you laugh?
How would you react if the same technique were used against your own side?

scenario.title: Meme campaign
scenario.situation: For months, social media is flooded with memes showing a politician with a distorted face. No arguments are made — only mockery.
scenario.question: What effect does this have?

options.explanation: It makes criticizing the politician socially costly.
Systematic satire has measurable effects on public perception.

options.text: If it's funny, there must be some truth to it
options.explanation: Humor can be effective even when entirely misleading.

name: Demonization
subtitle: The Absolute Enemy
summary: Portraying the opponent as the embodiment of evil, stripped of any human qualities.
definition: Demonization represents the opponent not as someone with different opinions, but as a force of evil — an existential threat, a being devoid of morality or humanity.
historicalContext: A central technique in every genocide: Jews, Tutsis, and Armenians were first demonized as 'rats,' 'cockroaches,' and 'parasites.' Demonization psychologically prepares populations for violence.
psychologicalMechanism: It dehumanizes the enemy, removing moral inhibitions against violence. When someone is framed as 'pure evil,' any action taken against them becomes justified.

variants.name: Subhuman
variants.desc: Comparisons to animals, parasites, or disease

variants.name: Existential threat
variants.desc: 'They want to destroy everything we love'

variants.name: Conspiracy
variants.desc: 'They are everywhere, they control everything'

variants.name: Morally inferior
variants.desc: 'They have no values, they are savages'

redFlags: Comparisons to animals or diseases
Portrayal as an existential threat
Denial of humanity to the opponent
Language implying a need for 'purification' or 'elimination'

caseStudies.title: Radio Rwanda
caseStudies.description: Before the genocide, Tutsis were called 'inyenzi' (cockroaches). The demonization prepared the population for mass slaughter.
caseStudies.lesson: Demonization is the first step toward mass violence.

caseStudies.title: Nazi propaganda
caseStudies.description: Jews were depicted as rats 'infesting' society. Stripping away their humanity enabled the 'final solution.'
caseStudies.lesson: Words precede actions.

defense.action: Notice when the opponent loses human attributes in the discourse
Demonization has preceded every genocide
No group is monolithic or absolutely evil
Neither use nor accept terms that dehumanize

scenario.title: Public speech
scenario.situation: A speaker says: 'These parasites won't stop until they have destroyed everything we love. They are a plague that must be eradicated.'
scenario.question: Which technique do you recognize?

options.text: Demonization: dehumanizes the group as parasites to be eradicated
options.explanation: Language that prepares for violence by stripping away humanity.
'Parasites' and 'eradicated' are dehumanizing terms.
History shows that this type of language precedes violence.

name: Firehose of Falsehood
subtitle: Flood of Lies
summary: Flooding the information space with such a high volume of contradictory messages that fact-checking becomes impossible.
definition: A propaganda model that bombards the public with many false and contradictory messages — betting that corrections cannot keep pace.
historicalContext: Theorized by the RAND Corporation in 2016 to describe Russian operations during the annexation of Crimea. Unlike Soviet propaganda, it openly accepts obvious contradictions.
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits the 'illusory truth effect' (repetition makes things seem true) and 'cognitive fatigue' (too many contradictory pieces of information lead people to stop verifying).

variants.name: High volume
variants.desc: More information than can possibly be verified

variants.name: Speed
variants.desc: Rapid publication before corrections can be issued

variants.name: Incoherence
variants.desc: Accepts obvious contradictions

variants.name: Multiple channels
variants.desc: Deployed across different platforms simultaneously

redFlags: A continuous flow of stories that contradict previous narratives
Stories that change their version rapidly
Contradictory narratives occupying the same information space

caseStudies.title: MH17
caseStudies.description: After the plane was shot down, 15 different explanations were proposed within days: a Ukrainian missile, a fighter jet, a CIA plot...
caseStudies.lesson: A plurality of explanations can be more effective than a single lie.

defense.action: Do not react in real time
Seek confirmation from independent sources
Recognize multiple and contradictory narratives

scenario.title: International crisis
scenario.situation: Within 48 hours: 'There are no troops' (Mon), 'Defensive exercises' (Tue morning), 'The enemy provoked us' (Tue afternoon), 'We were attacked' (Tue evening).
scenario.question: Which pattern do you recognize?

options.text: Firehose: contradictory narratives in rapid succession
options.explanation: Obvious contradictions within 48 hours.
Coherent accounts do not change this radically.
The pattern is characteristic of the firehose technique.

name: Chain Letter
subtitle: Viral Amplification
summary: Exploiting virality to amplify unverified or false information.
definition: The modern chain letter exploits social media algorithms to amplify emotionally charged content, regardless of its truthfulness.
historicalContext: Originating as paper chain letters, the format evolved alongside social media. Algorithms reward engagement, not accuracy.
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits social proof and emotion: content that generates anger or fear is shared more rapidly.

variants.name: Emotional chain
variants.desc: 'Share if you care!'

variants.name: Alert
variants.desc: 'Urgent warning for everyone!'

variants.name: Luck chain
variants.desc: 'Share or something bad will happen to you'

variants.name: Viral petition
variants.desc: False or manipulated petitions

redFlags: Explicit call to share
Highly emotional content with no sources
Threats or promises tied to sharing

caseStudies.title: WhatsApp chains
caseStudies.description: During epidemics and elections, chains of unverified messages spread exponentially, bypassing fact-checkers.
caseStudies.lesson: The speed of sharing outpaces the speed of verification.

defense.action: Do not forward automatically
Seek independent sources
Reply with a fact-check when possible

scenario.title: Viral message
scenario.situation: You receive: 'URGENT! New banking scam! Share with all your contacts to protect them!' No sources provided.
scenario.question: What do you do?

options.explanation: The urgency is artificial; verification is always possible.

options.text: I share immediately to protect people
options.explanation: The message itself could be a scam or disinformation.

options.text: I share with a note saying 'unverified'
options.explanation: Even with a disclaimer, you are amplifying unverified content.

name: Framing
subtitle: Narrative Framing
summary: The facts do not change — only the frame through which they are presented.
definition: Framing selects certain aspects of reality and makes them more salient, promoting a particular definition of the problem, causal interpretation, and moral evaluation.
historicalContext: A concept derived from media sociology (Goffman, Entman). The same facts can be presented through entirely different frames.
psychologicalMechanism: Operates through accessibility (information presented first influences interpretation) and applicability (the frame activates specific mental schemas).

variants.name: Conflict frame
variants.desc: Presenting an issue as a clash between opposing sides

variants.name: Economic frame
variants.desc: Evaluating in terms of costs and benefits

variants.name: Moral frame
variants.desc: Issues of values, good vs. evil

variants.name: Security frame
variants.desc: Presenting an issue as a threat

redFlags: The same event described with very different language by different sources
Terms that are systematically emotionally charged
Selective focus on certain aspects of a story

caseStudies.title: Immigration
caseStudies.description: Outlet A: 'security crisis' (invasion, illegal aliens). Outlet B: 'humanitarian emergency' (refugees, hope).
caseStudies.lesson: You do not need to lie: selection alone constructs different realities.

defense.action: How is this event being presented?
Are the terms neutral or emotionally loaded?
How is this event being presented elsewhere?

scenario.title: Street protest
scenario.situation: 10,000 people demonstrate; 50 cause disturbances. Outlet A: 'VIOLENCE IN THE CITY.' Outlet B: 'TEN THOUSAND TAKE TO THE STREETS.'
scenario.question: What distinguishes the two approaches?

options.text: Different framing: same facts, opposing frames
options.explanation: Both are true, but each selects different aspects of the event.
Both could be factually correct.
Outlet A conceals the 9,950 peaceful protesters.

name: Whataboutism
subtitle: The Deflection
summary: Deflecting every criticism by pointing to the faults of others instead of responding.
definition: A variant of the 'tu quoque' fallacy: instead of responding to a criticism, one accuses the critic of the same behavior.
historicalContext: Perfected by Soviet propaganda. When the West criticized human rights conditions in the USSR, the standard reply was: 'And you? You have racism!'
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits the desire for moral consistency: we feel uncomfortable criticizing someone if we ourselves are not perfect.

variants.name: Direct whatabout
variants.desc: 'What about you? You do the same thing!'

variants.name: Historical whatabout
variants.desc: 'What about what you did in 1950?'

variants.name: Systemic whatabout
variants.desc: 'What about capitalism?'

variants.name: Selective whatabout
variants.desc: Only citing cases that favor one's own narrative

redFlags: A response that does not address the question
'Yes, but...' followed by others' faults
Comparisons used to justify anything

caseStudies.title: Soviet responses
caseStudies.description: Criticisms about political dissidents were met with: 'What about Black Americans?' Legitimate criticisms were weaponized to shut down debate.
caseStudies.lesson: Valid criticisms can be used as shields to deflect other valid criticisms.

defense.action: 'Did you answer the question or just ask another one?'
'Can we discuss these two issues separately?'
'Now: is this particular decision right or wrong?'

scenario.title: Difficult interview
scenario.situation: Journalist: 'Your party was convicted of illegal financing.' Politician: 'And what about Party X? They did worse!'
scenario.question: How should one respond?

options.text: 'We can discuss everything, but right now: what is your response to the conviction?'
options.explanation: This prevents the question from being replaced.

options.text: Engage with the discussion about other parties
options.explanation: That is exactly what whataboutism is designed to achieve.
Moral equivalence is the intended outcome.

name: Agenda Setting
subtitle: Control of the Invisible
summary: What matters is not how we talk about it, but WHAT we do not talk about.
definition: The capacity of media to influence not what the public thinks, but what the public thinks about.
historicalContext: Theory formulated by McCombs and Shaw in 1972: the media do not tell people what to think, but what to think about.
psychologicalMechanism: The brain assesses relevance based on media salience. If something is widely discussed, it must be important.

variants.name: Selective amplification
variants.desc: Some topics receive constant coverage; others receive none

variants.name: Selective timing
variants.desc: Inconvenient news released at low-attention moments

variants.name: Gatekeeping
variants.desc: Simply not allowing certain topics to pass through

variants.name: Competitive agenda
variants.desc: Saturating the media with irrelevant topics

redFlags: Scandals receiving disproportionate coverage
Important topics receiving zero attention
News 'buried' in low-traffic time slots

caseStudies.title: Saturation
caseStudies.description: During weeks of scandal coverage, important legislation passes unnoticed.
caseStudies.lesson: What is not said is more important than what is said.

defense.action: 'What is NOT being talked about?'
Different sources have different agendas
Read minor sections and less prominent stories too

scenario.title: A media week
scenario.situation: For a week, 15 of 20 minutes of airtime are devoted to a celebrity accused of fraud. A major electoral reform is passed with 30 seconds of coverage aired at night.
scenario.question: Which phenomenon do you observe?

options.text: Selective agenda setting: focus on entertainment, silence on politics
options.explanation: The reform affects millions; the celebrity scandal affects no one in any material way.

options.text: Normal, the celebrity is more interesting
options.explanation: It seems interesting because it is presented as important.
Electoral reforms have effects that last decades.

name: Poisoning the Well
subtitle: Preemptive Delegitimization
summary: Pre-judging a source before it can speak, making it impossible to receive its arguments fairly.
definition: Presenting negative information about a source BEFORE that source has had a chance to speak, so that anything it says is filtered through a negative lens.
historicalContext: The term comes from the ancient practice of literally poisoning wells. In rhetoric, it is a preemptive form of attack.
psychologicalMechanism: The primacy effect: the first piece of information received about someone shapes the interpretation of everything that follows.

variants.name: Pre-defamation
variants.desc: Releasing 'negative information' before a speech or appearance

variants.name: Preventive association
variants.desc: 'They are about to speak, but remember that...'

variants.name: Preemptive disqualification
variants.desc: 'Everything they say will be false because...'

redFlags: Negative information presented before the source has spoken
Warnings to 'not trust X' issued ahead of events
Systematically negative qualifiers attached to a source

caseStudies.title: Political debates
caseStudies.description: Before a debate, negative stories about a candidate are circulated so that every word the candidate says is received with suspicion.
caseStudies.lesson: Preemptive damage is difficult to repair.

defense.action: Is the negative information arriving BEFORE the source speaks?
Evaluate the arguments, not just the source
Is the preemptively circulated information actually true?

scenario.title: Pre-event
scenario.situation: Before an expert's conference, the following circulates: 'Warning: this person has ties to interested industries!' No specifics are given, nor is there any indication of whether such ties influence the research.
scenario.question: What do you recognize?

options.text: Poisoning the well: preemptive prejudice to shape how the expert is received
options.explanation: The vague information arrives BEFORE the talk, to color everything that follows.
Without specifics, it is just prejudice.
Genuine transparency specifies the ties; vague accusations are not transparency.

name: Card Stacking
subtitle: Stacking the Deck
summary: Presenting only the facts favorable to one's thesis while concealing those that contradict it.
definition: Building an entire message from true facts that are selectively chosen and presented in a one-sided manner.
historicalContext: Term coined by the Institute for Propaganda Analysis (1937). Distinct from lying: the facts are true but incomplete.
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits confirmation bias and the illusion of completeness: when we see a lot of data, we assume the picture is complete.

variants.name: Omission
variants.desc: Simply not mentioning contrary facts

variants.name: Selective emphasis
variants.desc: Giving much space to favorable facts, little or none to unfavorable ones

variants.name: Missing context
variants.desc: True facts without the context that would change their meaning

redFlags: A very one-sided presentation
Absence of data that could contradict the thesis
All sources sharing the same orientation

caseStudies.title: Corporate reports
caseStudies.description: Annual reports that celebrate successes but make no mention of failures or risks.
caseStudies.lesson: Completeness requires including what we do not like.

defense.action: 'What data contradicts this thesis?'
What do those with different interests say?
What is NOT being said?

scenario.title: Sales report
scenario.situation: A company announces: 'We sold 1 million units!' It does not mention that 3 million were projected.
scenario.question: Which technique is this?

options.text: Card stacking: a true fact but with hidden context
options.explanation: The million units are real, but the figure is incomplete without comparison to projections.

options.text: Fake news — the figure is false
options.explanation: The figure could be entirely true.
Omitting context creates a misleading impression.

name: Labeling
subtitle: Name-Calling
summary: Using labels to define people, groups, or ideas — simplifying and often delegitimizing them.
definition: Applying labels (positive or negative) that replace argument and short-circuit critical thinking.
historicalContext: Systematically used in every conflict: 'terrorist' vs. 'freedom fighter,' 'patriot' vs. 'nationalist.'
psychologicalMechanism: Labels activate stereotypes and bypass analysis. Once a label is applied, we stop analyzing the content.

variants.name: Demonizing labels
variants.desc: 'Traitor,' 'enemy,' 'subversive'

variants.name: Ennobling labels
variants.desc: 'Hero,' 'pioneer,' 'defender'

variants.name: Reductive labels
variants.desc: 'Right-wing,' 'left-wing' used to shut down debate

redFlags: Labels used instead of arguments
Labels substituting for analysis
Systematic labeling applied to certain groups

caseStudies.title: The Cold War
caseStudies.description: 'Communist' was used to delegitimize any progressive position without engaging with its merits.
caseStudies.lesson: Labels shut down debate.

defense.action: 'Is this a label or an argument?'
'What exactly does this label mean in this specific context?'
Reality is more complex than any label

scenario.title: Debate
scenario.situation: Someone proposes a tax reform. Their opponent says: 'That's typical socialist thinking!' No discussion of the actual proposal.
scenario.question: What is happening?

options.text: Labeling: the argument is replaced with a label
options.explanation: The label closes off debate about the actual proposal.
There is no analysis — just a label.
Without argument, it is just a conversation-stopper.

name: Bandwagon
subtitle: Herd Effect
summary: 'Everyone is doing it!' — Exploiting the desire for social conformity.
definition: A technique that exploits social proof: if 'everyone' does or believes something, it must be right, true, or desirable.
historicalContext: One of the seven main techniques identified by the Institute for Propaganda Analysis in 1937. Used in advertising and politics.
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits the need to belong and the fear of being excluded. We tend to conform to what we perceive as the social norm.

variants.name: Explicit bandwagon
variants.desc: 'Everyone is doing it!' / 'Thousands of people...'

variants.name: Implicit bandwagon
variants.desc: Showing cheering crowds and long queues

variants.name: False consensus
variants.desc: Inflating support numbers

redFlags: Invitations to join the majority
Unverifiable support numbers
Implicit social pressure

caseStudies.title: The 1936 election
caseStudies.description: The Literary Digest predicted a Landon victory based on millions of questionnaire responses — but the sample was skewed. The bandwagon turned out to be wrong.
caseStudies.lesson: The majority can be wrong.

defense.action: Is the 'everyone' real or imaginary?
Even if everyone did it, would it be right?
Majorities have often supported things that were wrong

scenario.title: Advertising campaign
scenario.situation: 'Used by millions!' No specification of how many millions, or whether the product actually works.
scenario.question: Which technique is this?

options.text: Bandwagon: vague social proof to induce conformity
options.explanation: Vague numbers are used to suggest consensus.
Without specific data, it is mere suggestion.
It says nothing about the quality of the product.

name: Glittering Generalities
subtitle: Shining Words
summary: Using words with positive connotations but no specific meaning.
definition: Words such as 'freedom,' 'democracy,' 'progress,' and 'justice' used without defining their content, in order to associate positive feelings with a cause.
historicalContext: Identified by the Institute for Propaganda Analysis. Complementary to Name-Calling: empty positive words instead of negative ones.
psychologicalMechanism: Positive words activate positive emotions without activating critical thinking. After all, who is against 'freedom'?

variants.name: Abstract values
variants.desc: 'Freedom,' 'justice,' 'progress'

variants.name: Empty qualifiers
variants.desc: 'The right solution,' 'real change'

variants.name: Positive adjectives
variants.desc: 'Courageous,' 'visionary,' 'authentic'

redFlags: Words with strong emotional charge but no definition
Everyone can agree on them, but they mean different things to different people
Statements that sound good but say nothing

caseStudies.title: Electoral campaigns
caseStudies.description: 'For freedom!' 'For real progress!' Without defining what these words mean in the actual platform.
caseStudies.lesson: Everyone loves freedom, but people define it differently.

defense.action: 'What does freedom actually mean in this program?'
Behind the glittering word, what is being concretely proposed?
What is NOT being said?

scenario.title: Political slogan
scenario.situation: 'Vote for real freedom and real progress!' No explanation of what 'real' means or what concrete policies are proposed.
scenario.question: What do you recognize?

options.text: Glittering generalities: positive but empty words with no content
options.explanation: Everyone loves freedom and progress, but without definitions they are meaningless.
Without content, it is only emotional suggestion.
It does not actually inform about any content.

name: Transfer
subtitle: Authority Transfer
summary: Transferring the authority or prestige of a figure or symbol to a cause.
definition: Associating a cause with respected (or despised) symbols, people, or institutions to automatically transfer those feelings.
historicalContext: Used systematically in advertising (celebrity endorsement) and politics (photographs with flags, religious figures, military personnel).
psychologicalMechanism: The brain automatically transfers feelings from the source to the associated object. If a respected person supports X, then X acquires respectability.

variants.name: Religious authority
variants.desc: Associating causes with religious figures or sacred symbols

variants.name: Scientific authority
variants.desc: Citing experts outside their field of expertise

variants.name: National symbols
variants.desc: Flags, anthems, monuments used to legitimize causes

variants.name: Moral authority
variants.desc: Citing respected historical figures

redFlags: Association with authoritative figures who are out of context
Use of sacred or national symbols for ordinary causes
Experts cited outside their field of competence

caseStudies.title: Scientific advertising
caseStudies.description: An actor who plays a doctor on TV recommends a medication. The transferred authority is fictional but effective.
caseStudies.lesson: Authority must be relevant and genuine.

defense.action: Does this authority have expertise ON THIS specific topic?
Does the cause have its own merits, independent of who supports it?
Is a symbol being used to legitimize something unrelated?

scenario.title: Endorsement
scenario.situation: A famous actor takes a position on global economic policy. He has no economics training.
scenario.question: Which technique is this?

options.text: Transfer: the actor's prestige is transferred to the economic position
options.explanation: Fame is not economic expertise.
Without relevant expertise, it is only a prestige transfer.

options.text: Everyone is entitled to an opinion
options.explanation: True, but using fame to legitimize a position is transfer.

name: Testimonial
subtitle: Personal Testimony
summary: Using personal testimonials instead of data or arguments.
definition: Presenting individual experiences as general proof, or using paid or coached witnesses to reinforce a message.
historicalContext: The foundation of modern advertising. In propaganda, used to humanize causes or delegitimize opponents.
psychologicalMechanism: Personal stories are more persuasive than abstract data. The brain remembers narratives better than statistics.

variants.name: Emotional testimony
variants.desc: Tear-jerking stories that bypass data

variants.name: Authority testimony
variants.desc: Celebrities or experts cited outside their field

variants.name: Orchestrated testimony
variants.desc: Coached or paid witnesses

redFlags: Moving stories without supporting data
A single experience presented as the norm
Witnesses with hidden interests

caseStudies.title: 'Maria the housewife'
caseStudies.description: Advertisements featuring 'real consumers' who turn out to be actors. The testimony appears spontaneous but is scripted.
caseStudies.lesson: Always verify the authenticity and interests of the witness.

defense.action: What do the statistics say, not just the stories?
Do they have interests? Are they representative?
An anecdote is not data; a story is not a statistic

scenario.title: Health campaign
scenario.situation: 'My cousin took this medication and was cured!' Presented as proof of effectiveness, without any clinical studies.
scenario.question: What do you recognize?

options.text: Testimonial: an anecdote presented as general proof
options.explanation: A single case is not a statistic.
Without statistical context, it is misleading.
Legitimate as personal experience, not as scientific proof.

name: Repetition
subtitle: Repetition
summary: Repeating a message until it is accepted as true.
definition: The constant repetition of a claim makes it seem true, regardless of its actual truthfulness.
historicalContext: 'A lie repeated a thousand times becomes truth' (attributed to Goebbels). This effect is studied in psychology as the 'illusory truth effect.'
psychologicalMechanism: The brain uses ease of processing as a proxy for truth. If something is familiar, it seems true.

variants.name: Explicit repetition
variants.desc: The same slogan repeated obsessively

variants.name: Multi-channel repetition
variants.desc: The same message across multiple platforms

variants.name: Repetition with variations
variants.desc: The same concept formulated in different ways

redFlags: The same message appearing everywhere
Slogans repeated without argument
Claims that become 'common knowledge' without evidence

caseStudies.title: The illusory truth effect
caseStudies.description: Studies show that statements presented multiple times are judged to be more true — even by people who had previously classified them as false.
caseStudies.lesson: Familiarity is not truth.

defense.action: Have I heard this many times?
Familiarity is not proof
A lie repeated is still a lie

scenario.title: Ubiquitous slogan
scenario.situation: For months, every channel repeats: 'The problem is X!' No data is presented, but everyone 'knows' it is true.
scenario.question: What effect does this have?

options.explanation: Familiarity creates the illusion of truth.
Repetition does not create truth.
Without data, it is only repetition.

name: Slogan
subtitle: Short Phrases
summary: Short, memorable phrases that replace complex thought.
definition: Short, easy-to-remember and easy-to-repeat formulas that summarize (and often drastically oversimplify) a position.
historicalContext: 'Make America Great Again,' 'Hope and Change,' 'Yes We Can.' Slogans are central to every political campaign.
psychologicalMechanism: The brain prefers easily processed information. Slogans are memorized automatically and recalled with ease.

variants.name: Identity slogan
variants.desc: '[Country] First!' / 'Citizens, not subjects'

variants.name: Action slogan
variants.desc: 'Enough [X]!' / 'Yes we can!'

variants.name: Contrasting slogan
variants.desc: 'They destroy, we build'

redFlags: Very short phrases summarizing complex issues
Easy to chant, impossible to debate
Used in place of arguments

caseStudies.title: Slogans in dictatorship
caseStudies.description: 'One people, one empire, one leader' — three words that summarize and simplify a complex totalitarian ideology.
caseStudies.lesson: Slogans bypass critical thinking.

defense.action: 'What does this slogan actually mean in concrete terms?'
Behind the simple phrase, what is the actual program?
What does the slogan NOT say?

scenario.title: Campaign rally
scenario.situation: The candidate shouts: 'Law and order!' The crowd cheers. There is no discussion of which laws or what kind of order.
scenario.question: Which technique is this?

options.text: Slogan: a formula that replaces substance
options.explanation: Three words for complex issues.
Nothing specific is stated.
A summary without content is just a slogan.

name: Plain Folks
subtitle: Ordinary People
summary: Presenting oneself as 'one of you' in order to gain trust.
definition: A technique in which those who hold power present themselves as ordinary people in order to build identification and trust.
historicalContext: Identified by the Institute for Propaganda Analysis in 1937. Used by political leaders across every era.
psychologicalMechanism: We tend to trust those we perceive as similar to ourselves. If the leader is 'just like us,' they must have our interests at heart.

variants.name: Staged events
variants.desc: Politicians 'doing their grocery shopping' only during election campaigns

variants.name: Artificial language
variants.desc: Educated people deliberately using colloquial expressions

variants.name: Humble origins
variants.desc: Emphasis on 'modest' origins now long in the past

redFlags: Politicians appearing in 'everyday' settings only during campaigns
Language carefully crafted to sound informal
Emphasis on humble origins that no longer reflect current reality

caseStudies.title: Joe the Plumber
caseStudies.description: He became a symbol of the ordinary citizen. Investigations revealed he was not a licensed plumber and his question had been prepared in advance.
caseStudies.lesson: Even real people can be used as orchestrated instruments.

defense.action: Is the 'ordinariness' genuine or performed?
What have they actually done, not just how do they appear?
Is the 'everyday' context spontaneous or prepared?

scenario.title: Viral video
scenario.situation: A video shows 'Maria, 42, homemaker' complaining about the government. It is professionally filmed, with no surname or city given.
scenario.question: What should you verify?

options.text: Identity, hidden interests, the nature of the production
options.explanation: Professional quality and anonymity suggest orchestration.

options.text: She is a citizen expressing her opinion
options.explanation: She may be, but it needs to be verified.

options.text: No need to verify — she's just an ordinary person
options.explanation: The professional anonymous video warrants caution.

name: Appeal to Authority
subtitle: Appeal to Authority
summary: Citing authorities as proof, even when they are irrelevant or not competent on the topic.
definition: A fallacy that cites an authority as definitive proof, even when the authority has no specific expertise on the subject.
historicalContext: Aristotle classified appeal to authority as a fallacy when the authority is not relevant to the question at hand.
psychologicalMechanism: Respect for authority is deeply internalized culturally. When an authority speaks, we tend to accept without verifying.

variants.name: Out-of-field authority
variants.desc: A doctor speaking about economics

variants.name: Dubious authority
variants.desc: Citing sources with conflicts of interest

variants.name: Lone authority
variants.desc: A single voice against scientific consensus

redFlags: Experts cited outside their field
One authority against the consensus
Authorities with hidden conflicts of interest

caseStudies.title: Tobacco and doctors
caseStudies.description: For decades, the industry cited doctors who downplayed the risks of smoking. They were paid to do so.
caseStudies.lesson: Verify the competence, relevance, and interests of the authority.

defense.action: Does this person have expertise ON THIS specific topic?
Is this an isolated voice or the scientific consensus?
Do they have conflicts of interest?

scenario.title: Campaign
scenario.situation: '9 out of 10 scientists say X is safe!' No specification of which scientists, whether they have relevant expertise, or whether they have interests.
scenario.question: What questions should you ask?

options.text: Competence, conflicts of interest, representativeness of the sample
options.explanation: Authority without context is not proof.

options.text: If 9 out of 10 say so, it must be true
options.explanation: It depends on who the 10 are.
Especially when speaking outside their own field.

name: Polarization
subtitle: Us vs. Them
summary: Constructing antagonistic identities that make dialogue impossible.
definition: A strategy of dividing society into two mutually exclusive camps, where group membership matters more than truth.
historicalContext: Used by regimes to consolidate power. In the digital age, polarizing content receives more engagement.
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits tribalism: the brain processes information differently depending on whether it comes from 'our group' or 'the enemy.'

variants.name: Military terminology
variants.desc: 'Battle,' 'front' applied to politics

variants.name: Total delegitimization
variants.desc: 'They have no right to speak'

variants.name: Apocalypticism
variants.desc: 'If they win, it's the end'

redFlags: Debate shifted from facts to identity
Systematic 'us' vs. 'them' framing
Internal criticism treated as betrayal

caseStudies.title: The algorithm of division
caseStudies.description: Content that generates outrage toward the out-group receives more shares. This is a business model.
caseStudies.lesson: Polarization is also an economic model.

defense.action: When you feel like part of a 'team,' stop and reflect
Which intermediate positions are being excluded?
That is the first step out of polarization

scenario.title: Speech
scenario.situation: 'Whoever is not with us is against us! There is no middle ground!'
scenario.question: Which mechanism is at work?

options.text: Elimination of the center: only allies or enemies
options.explanation: Constructs a rigid identity with no nuance.
Motivating people does not require eliminating intermediate positions.
Normalizing apocalypticism is part of the problem.

name: Gish Gallop
subtitle: Flood of Claims
summary: Too many claims delivered too quickly for all of them to be verified.
definition: Bombarding an opponent with so many unverified claims that refuting them all is impossible.
historicalContext: Named after creationist Duane Gish, who would fire off dozens of arguments in a matter of minutes.
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits asymmetry: making a claim takes seconds; refuting it takes minutes or hours.

variants.name: Video marathon
variants.desc: Hours of content containing hundreds of claims

variants.name: Infinite thread
variants.desc: Dozens of unverified points in a social media thread

variants.name: Document dump
variants.desc: Links to massive PDFs: 'It's all in there!'

redFlags: Each point addressed in 10–30 seconds
No sources provided for individual claims
Machine-gun structure of argumentation

caseStudies.title: Creationist debates
caseStudies.description: Gish would launch dozens of arguments in 5 minutes. Refuting one takes 10 minutes. The audience saw claims going 'unrefuted.'
caseStudies.lesson: The quantity of arguments impresses non-experts.

defense.action: Choose only the most important points to address
'You are making 50 claims in 5 minutes'
If some turn out to be false, the rest is probably unreliable too

scenario.title: Video
scenario.situation: A 45-minute video lists 80 alleged scandals, one after another, without sources.
scenario.question: How do you approach this?

options.explanation: If they are false, the rest of the content is likely unreliable.
45 minutes of unverified claims are not worth more than 1 minute.

options.text: With so many points, some must be true
options.explanation: That is the error the technique is designed to induce.

name: Straw Man
subtitle: Scarecrow
summary: Distorting the opponent's argument in order to attack it more easily.
definition: Constructing a weak or distorted version of the opponent's argument, attacking that version, and pretending to have refuted the original.
historicalContext: The name comes from the ease of knocking down a straw man. It is one of the most common logical fallacies.
psychologicalMechanism: The audience perceives that the argument has been 'refuted,' without noticing that the argument actually attacked was not the original one.

variants.name: Oversimplification
variants.desc: Reducing a complex position to a trivial phrase

variants.name: Extremization
variants.desc: Presenting a moderate position as extreme

variants.name: Fabrication
variants.desc: Attributing to the opponent positions they never held

redFlags: The opponent's position is presented in a ridiculous or extreme way
The attack is easy but does not reflect what was actually said
The opponent says 'But I never said that!'

caseStudies.title: Political debates
caseStudies.description: Candidate A proposes modest tax reforms. Candidate B: 'They want to destroy the economy!' They are attacking a position A never held.
caseStudies.lesson: Always check: is the argument being attacked actually the one that was made?

defense.action: 'Is that really what I argued?'
'I never said that. My actual position is...'
Do not defend a position that is not yours

scenario.title: Debate
scenario.situation: You say: 'We should reduce taxes slightly.' Your opponent: 'You want to eliminate all public services!'
scenario.question: Which technique is this?

options.text: Straw man: a moderate proposal is distorted into an extreme one
options.explanation: Attacks a position that was never stated.
It does not address the actual proposal.
It is not a consequence — it is a distortion.

name: Red Herring
subtitle: Smoked Herring
summary: Distracting with an irrelevant argument.
definition: Introducing an irrelevant piece of information in order to distract from the main topic.
historicalContext: The name comes from the use of smoked herrings to confuse hunting dogs, distracting them from the trail.
psychologicalMechanism: The brain follows salient information. By introducing an exciting topic, attention is diverted from the main issue.

variants.name: Emotional distraction
variants.desc: Shifting to a topic that generates a strong reaction

variants.name: Whatabout
variants.desc: 'What about this other problem?'

variants.name: New topic
variants.desc: Changing the subject entirely

redFlags: The argument shifts suddenly
An unrelated topic is introduced
The answer has nothing to do with the question

caseStudies.title: Interviews
caseStudies.description: Journalist: 'What do you say about these scandals?' Politician: 'But look how wonderful our education reform is!'
caseStudies.lesson: Changing the subject is not an answer.

defense.action: 'This doesn't answer the question'
Repeat the original question
Do not let yourself be dragged onto the new topic

scenario.title: Interview
scenario.situation: Journalist: 'What about the corruption investigation?' Politician: 'We should be talking about our economy, which is doing great!'
scenario.question: Which technique is this?

options.text: Red herring: distraction with an irrelevant topic
options.explanation: The economy does not address the corruption question.
It does not answer the question.
It is not related to what was asked.

name: Projection
subtitle: Projection
summary: Accusing others of exactly what you are doing.
definition: A technique in which exactly what one is doing is attributed to one's opponents, thereby neutralizing accusations.
historicalContext: Used systematically by dictatorships and authoritarian regimes: they accuse the opposition of being 'coup plotters' while planning a coup themselves.
psychologicalMechanism: Confuses the public ('who is right?') and pre-builds a defense ('they are saying the same thing'). Renders accusations mutually canceling.

variants.name: Mirror accusation
variants.desc: 'You're the one doing X!' — while doing it yourself

variants.name: Pre-accusation
variants.desc: Accusing before being accused

variants.name: Inversion
variants.desc: Presenting the victim as the aggressor

redFlags: Accusations that seem perfectly mirrored
The accuser exhibits the same behavior
Suspicious timing of accusations

caseStudies.title: Authoritarian regimes
caseStudies.description: Regimes preparing electoral fraud accuse the opposition of 'planning fraud,' preemptively delegitimizing any accusations that will follow.
caseStudies.lesson: Mirror accusations must be verified on both sides.

defense.action: Do not assume 'the truth lies in the middle'
Who has evidence, and who only has accusations?
Do the accusations come before or after the behavior?

scenario.title: Debate
scenario.situation: A party accuses the other of 'censoring debate.' At the same time, it is expelling internal dissidents.
scenario.question: Which technique is this?

options.text: Projection: accusing others of what you are doing
options.explanation: Accuses others of censorship while itself censoring.
Not when the accuser is doing the same thing.
Projection is a deliberate technique, not mere equivalence.

name: Dog Whistle
subtitle: Coded Message
summary: Coded messages that only certain groups understand.
definition: Communications that carry an innocuous meaning for the general public but convey a specific message to a target group.
historicalContext: The term comes from dog whistles, audible only to dogs. Systematically used in politics to signal to extremist bases.
psychologicalMechanism: Allows controversial messages to be communicated while maintaining plausible deniability ('I didn't mean that').

variants.name: Historical codes
variants.desc: References recognized only by certain groups

variants.name: Loaded terms
variants.desc: Words with dual meanings

variants.name: Symbolism
variants.desc: Gestures, numbers, or images with specific in-group meaning

redFlags: Messages that seem innocuous but generate strong reactions in certain groups
Terms with 'secret meanings'
Systematic plausible deniability

caseStudies.title: Electoral campaigns
caseStudies.description: A candidate uses terms that seem innocuous but are recognized by extremist groups as signals of support. When criticized: 'I didn't mean that!'
caseStudies.lesson: Context and the reaction of the target audience reveal the true meaning.

defense.action: How are extremist groups responding?
Does the term have a dual meaning?
Is the denial plausible?

scenario.title: Speech
scenario.situation: A candidate uses terms that seem normal but are recognized by extremist groups as signals. When criticized: 'But I said something completely ordinary!'
scenario.question: Which technique is this?

options.text: Dog whistle: a coded message with plausible deniability
options.explanation: Two levels of meaning operating simultaneously.

options.text: An innocent phrase that was misunderstood
options.explanation: The target group's reaction reveals the true meaning.
The coordinated reaction of certain groups indicates comprehension of the code.

name: Gaslighting
subtitle: Denying Reality
summary: Making victims doubt their own perception of reality.
definition: A manipulation technique that denies evident facts, minimizes reactions, and makes victims doubt their own judgment.
historicalContext: The term comes from the film 'Gaslight' (1944), in which a husband manipulates his wife into believing she is going mad.
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits reliance on external confirmation. When constantly contradicted, one begins to doubt oneself.

variants.name: Denial
variants.desc: 'That never happened!'

variants.name: Minimization
variants.desc: 'You're overreacting!'

variants.name: Inversion
variants.desc: 'You're the one with problems!'

variants.name: Selective memory
variants.desc: 'I don't remember saying that'

redFlags: Constant denial of evident facts
Phrases like 'you're too sensitive' or 'you imagined it all'
The victim begins to doubt their own memory

caseStudies.title: Abusive relationships
caseStudies.description: An abusive partner denies episodes of violence, minimizes the victim's reactions, and makes the victim feel 'crazy.'
caseStudies.lesson: The systematic denial of reality is a form of violence.

defense.action: Keep records of facts and conversations
Seek confirmation from third parties
Systematic denial is a technique, not a misunderstanding

scenario.title: Conversation
scenario.situation: You say: 'Yesterday you said X.' They reply: 'I never said that! You imagined everything. You're paranoid.' It really did happen.
scenario.question: Which technique is this?

options.text: Gaslighting: denying facts to make you doubt yourself
options.explanation: Systematic denial of reality.
When it is systematic, it is a technique.
Total denial constitutes gaslighting.

name: Cherry-Picking
subtitle: Data Selection
summary: Selecting only favorable data while ignoring everything else.
definition: Selecting evidence that confirms a position while ignoring evidence that refutes it.
historicalContext: The term comes from the idea of picking only the best cherries. Used to delay the recognition of risks (tobacco, climate change).
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits confirmation bias: we find information that confirms our existing beliefs more credible.

variants.name: Truncated time series
variants.desc: Showing only a favorable time period

variants.name: Outliers as norm
variants.desc: Presenting an exceptional case as representative

variants.name: Out-of-context quotes
variants.desc: A phrase whose meaning changes when read in context

redFlags: Graphs without a complete time axis
A statistic cited without context
Absence of dissenting data

caseStudies.title: Tobacco
caseStudies.description: The industry cited studies showing uncertainty, ignoring the growing consensus.
caseStudies.lesson: Cherry-picking can create the illusion of debate.

defense.action: What does the complete historical series show?
Locate the original study
Which data are NOT being shown?

scenario.title: Press release
scenario.situation: 'Historic record! +150,000 new jobs!' No mention of the 200,000 jobs lost in the same period.
scenario.question: Which technique is this?

options.text: Cherry-picking: a favorable indicator without context
options.explanation: The figure is true but incomplete.

options.text: Fake news — the figure is false
options.explanation: The figure could be entirely true.
Omitting the negative creates a misleading impression.

name: Astroturfing
subtitle: Fake Grassroots Movement
summary: Simulating a bottom-up movement that is actually coordinated from the top.
definition: Creating movements, campaigns, or opinions that appear spontaneous but are actually orchestrated.
historicalContext: A term from the 1980s, when corporations created groups of 'concerned citizens' to oppose environmental regulations.
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits social proof: we tend to conform to what we perceive as the majority opinion.

variants.name: Bot networks
variants.desc: Coordinated automated accounts

variants.name: Troll farms
variants.desc: Centers where individuals manage dozens of fake accounts

variants.name: Review bombing
variants.desc: A wave of fake reviews

redFlags: Newly created accounts with sudden high activity
Nearly identical language across different profiles
Coordinated timing

caseStudies.title: IRA
caseStudies.description: The Internet Research Agency managed hundreds of accounts posing as American activists, amplifying divisions.
caseStudies.lesson: Astroturfing may aim only to undermine trust.

defense.action: Check creation date, number of posts, and consistency
Similar language, coordinated timing
Do not be impressed by numbers alone

scenario.title: Coordinated comments
scenario.situation: Under a critical post, 100 profiles appear with nearly identical phrases. Many have 3–10 posts total and were all created in the same month.
scenario.question: What does this indicate?

options.text: Probable astroturfing: new profiles, identical messages
options.explanation: All the classic warning signs are present.
Real citizens are not this coordinated.
We cannot draw conclusions from coordinated behavior alone.

name: False Flag
subtitle: Covert Operations
summary: Actions presented as coming from another party.
definition: Operations carried out in a way that makes them appear to have been executed by a different actor.
historicalContext: A naval term: sailing under a false flag. Historical examples include the Reichstag fire and the Gleiwitz incident.
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits the need to respond to perceived threats: if we believe we are being attacked by X, we support action against X.

variants.name: Clone sites
variants.desc: Replicas of real websites with false content

variants.name: Infiltrated accounts
variants.desc: Profiles pretending to belong to a group

variants.name: Provocations
variants.desc: Orchestrated protests designed to delegitimize

redFlags: An event that justifies actions already planned
Immediate attribution without investigation
A document that is 'too perfect'

caseStudies.title: Operation Northwoods
caseStudies.description: In 1962, US military chiefs proposed false flag operations against American civilians to be attributed to Cuba. The plan was rejected by Kennedy.
caseStudies.lesson: False flags are documented historical plans.

defense.action: Is the website the official one?
Do not accept immediate attributions
Who gains? Is it 'too convenient'?

scenario.title: Leaked document
scenario.situation: A PDF circulates: an internal memo containing cynical strategies. Anonymous account, no verifiable letterhead, published one week before an election.
scenario.question: How do you assess this?

options.text: Suspicious: anonymous source, suspicious timing, no possible verification
options.explanation: The combination suggests possible fabrication.

options.text: If it's online, it has been verified
options.explanation: Publication does not require verification.

options.text: It fits what I already believed, so it's credible
options.explanation: Confirmation bias is exactly what forgers exploit.

name: Deepfake
subtitle: Video Manipulation
summary: Creating artificial content indistinguishable from reality.
definition: Content produced with AI that replaces a person's face, voice, or movements.
historicalContext: The technology became accessible in 2017. Initially used for non-consensual pornographic content, it has since been applied to politics.
psychologicalMechanism: 'Seeing is believing': images carry greater perceived authority than text.

variants.name: Face swap
variants.desc: Replacement of the face

variants.name: Lip sync
variants.desc: Lip synchronization to alter speech

variants.name: Voice cloning
variants.desc: Replication of a person's voice

redFlags: Unnatural facial movements
Imperfect audio-visual synchronization
Graphical artifacts around the face or hair

caseStudies.title: Zelensky deepfake
caseStudies.description: A deepfake video showed Zelensky ordering surrender. The quality was imperfect but still required an official denial.
caseStudies.lesson: Political deepfakes are a present reality, not a future scenario.

defense.action: Where does this video come from?
Are the eyes, hands, and edges distorted?
For sensational videos, wait for independent confirmation

scenario.title: Sensational video
scenario.situation: A video shows a leader insulting voters. Source: anonymous account. Quality looks good but not perfect.
scenario.question: How do you approach this?

options.explanation: The combination warrants caution.

options.text: If it looks realistic, it must be real
options.explanation: Deepfakes are designed to look real.

options.text: I'll share it with a disclaimer
options.explanation: Even with a disclaimer, you are amplifying unverified content.

name: Influence Operations
subtitle: Information Warfare
summary: Coordinated campaigns to influence opinions and elections.
definition: Campaigns coordinated by states or other actors to shape public opinion and electoral outcomes.
historicalContext: Foreign interference has existed since the Cold War. The digital novelty is the scale and the ability to target citizens directly.
psychologicalMechanism: These operations amplify pre-existing divisions rather than creating new ones.

variants.name: Amplifying divisions
variants.desc: Promoting polarizing content

variants.name: Voter suppression
variants.desc: Discouraging certain segments from going to the polls

variants.name: Hack and leak
variants.desc: Stealing and selectively publishing material

redFlags: Sites that imitate existing media outlets
Identical narratives appearing in multiple languages simultaneously
Sources with large resources and zero transparency

caseStudies.title: Clone networks
caseStudies.description: Operations have created dozens of sites imitating European media outlets, publishing false articles.
caseStudies.lesson: Always check the exact domain.

defense.action: Is this the official website?
Has this site existed for a long time?
Content perfectly aligned with your own views deserves extra skepticism

scenario.title: Suspicious website
scenario.situation: 'TheNationalNews-Today.com' publishes an explosive exposé. The domain was registered two months ago, and the 'About Us' page lists no real names.
scenario.question: What should you verify?

options.explanation: A new domain, anonymity, and sensational content are all warning signs.
Even well-written content can be false.

options.text: The number of shares proves it is important
options.explanation: Shares do not prove truth.

name: Flooding
subtitle: Digital Flooding
summary: Drowning debate in noise and toxicity.
definition: Flooding comments, posts, and discussions with content that suffocates legitimate debate.
historicalContext: A central technique of modern 'troll farms.'
psychologicalMechanism: The noise exhausts those seeking serious information, who eventually disengage.

variants.name: Spam
variants.desc: Thousands of identical messages

variants.name: Coordinated trolling
variants.desc: Systematic insults and mockery

variants.name: Hashtag hijacking
variants.desc: Redirecting conversations by hijacking hashtags

redFlags: A disproportionate volume of negative comments
Accounts that appear only on certain topics
Coordinated timing

caseStudies.title: Organized commenting
caseStudies.description: In some countries, armies of commenters systematically attack critical voices online.
caseStudies.lesson: Volume does not reflect consensus.

defense.action: Don't feed the flood
Filters, blocks, and reports
Moderated platforms or threads

scenario.title: Invaded discussion
scenario.situation: A serious discussion is flooded by 500 insulting comments within a few hours. New accounts, similar messages.
scenario.question: Which technique is this?

options.explanation: Volume and coordination indicate an operation.
Genuine spontaneity is not this coordinated.
Volume is not consensus.

name: Fear Appeal
subtitle: Appeal to Fear
summary: Mobilizing through fear instead of argument.
definition: A technique that uses fear to induce action without critical thinking.
historicalContext: A cornerstone of advertising ('buy this or something bad will happen') and politics ('if not X, disaster will follow').
psychologicalMechanism: Fear activates the sympathetic nervous system, bypassing reflective thinking.

variants.name: Existential fear
variants.desc: 'If not X, it is the end'

variants.name: Personal fear
variants.desc: 'You or your loved ones...'

variants.name: Apocalypticism
variants.desc: Every alternative is presented as catastrophe

redFlags: Messages that generate intense anxiety
Extreme urgency
No analysis of alternatives

caseStudies.title: Fear-based advertising
caseStudies.description: Campaigns showing terrible consequences if you do not buy the product or service.
caseStudies.lesson: Fear sells, but it does not inform.

defense.action: Am I feeling anxious? That may be intentional.
Decisions made out of fear are often poor decisions
Reality is more nuanced than the apocalypse being described

scenario.title: Message
scenario.situation: 'SHARE NOW or your daughter could be in danger! You only have a few hours!'
scenario.question: Which technique is this?

options.text: Fear appeal + urgency: fear used to bypass critical thinking
options.explanation: Artificial fear and urgency are being weaponized.
Without sources, it is only manipulation.

options.text: Better to share it just in case
options.explanation: That is exactly what the technique is designed to make you do.

name: Flag-Waving
subtitle: Appeal to Nationalism
summary: Exploiting patriotism to legitimize causes.
definition: Associating a cause with the flag, the nation, and national symbols to render it beyond criticism.
historicalContext: A classic technique: those who oppose it 'do not love their country.'
psychologicalMechanism: National identity runs deep. Opposing something wrapped in the flag feels almost like an act of betrayal.

variants.name: National symbols
variants.desc: Flags, national anthem, national colors

variants.name: Patriotism vs. treason
variants.desc: Those who disagree are called 'anti-patriotic'

variants.name: History and glory
variants.desc: Invoking national greatness

redFlags: Arguments wrapped in the flag
Criticism equated with anti-patriotism
Excessive use of national symbols

caseStudies.title: The Iraq War
caseStudies.description: Criticism of the war was framed as 'not supporting the troops' or 'not loving America.'
caseStudies.lesson: One can love one's country and still criticize its actions.

defense.action: The cause and the country are two different things
Is this an argument or an emotional appeal?
Criticism does not equal treason

scenario.title: Debate
scenario.situation: Someone criticizes a government policy. Their opponent: 'If you don't like this country, leave!'
scenario.question: Which technique is this?

options.text: Flag-waving: patriotism used to shut down debate
options.explanation: Criticism is not anti-patriotism.
Criticism is a fundamental part of democracy.
It is only an emotional appeal.

name: Beautiful People
subtitle: Successful People
summary: Associating beauty and success with a cause.
definition: Showing attractive, happy, successful people associated with a product, candidate, or idea.
historicalContext: A cornerstone of modern advertising. Extended to politics.
psychologicalMechanism: Emotional transfer: if those people are happy and support X, then supporting X will make me happy too.

variants.name: Beauty
variants.desc: Attractive people associated with the cause

variants.name: Success
variants.desc: Wealthy or famous people who endorse it

variants.name: Happiness
variants.desc: Happy people using the product or voting for the candidate

redFlags: Images of attractive people with no connection to quality
Happiness associated with products or policies
Celebrities endorsing without relevant expertise

caseStudies.title: Political advertising
caseStudies.description: Candidates surrounded by young, attractive, happy people in their campaign spots.
caseStudies.lesson: The image says nothing about the proposals.

defense.action: What is being proposed, not who is proposing it
Is the person's attractiveness relevant to the issue?
Behind the image, what are the actual proposals?

scenario.title: Campaign
scenario.situation: A political ad shows beautiful, happy people dancing with the party logo. No policies are discussed.
scenario.question: Which technique is this?

options.text: Beautiful people: positive emotions associated with the party, no content
options.explanation: Beauty and happiness say nothing about actual proposals.
Without content, it is only emotional suggestion.
Marketing without substance is manipulation.
